fragments.content: Physical dependence and addiction are distinct concepts often confused. Physical dependence is a physiologic state where abrupt drug cessation causes withdrawal symptoms due to neuroadaptation. It develops predictably with chronic use of many medications (opioids, benzodiazepines, beta-blockers, clonidine, corticosteroids) and is expected, not pathological. Addiction (substance use disorder) is a chronic brain disease characterized by: compulsive use despite harm, craving, loss of control over use, continued use despite negative consequences, prioritizing drug use over other activities. Physical dependence can exist without addiction (cancer patient on opioids); addiction can exist without significant physical dependence (cocaine). The DSM-5 criteria for substance use disorder focus on behavioral patterns, not physiologic dependence.

Management differs fundamentally: Physical dependence requires gradual tapering to prevent withdrawal (opioid taper 10% per week; benzodiazepine taper over months using long-acting agent; beta-blocker taper over 1-2 weeks to prevent rebound hypertension/tachycardia). Addiction treatment involves: detoxification (managed withdrawal), rehabilitation (behavioral therapy, counseling), medication-assisted treatment (methadone/buprenorphine for opioid use disorder; naltrexone for alcohol/opioid; acamprosate for alcohol), long-term recovery support. Opioid example: cancer patient on chronic opioids has physical dependence - continue opioids, taper slowly if discontinuing. Patient seeking early refills, using multiple prescribers, IV use has addiction - refer for addiction treatment, consider MAT. Stigma reduction: avoid terms like 'abuse,' 'addict'; use 'substance use disorder,' 'person with addiction.'

DSM-5 Substance Use Disorder criteria (need ≥2 in 12 months): taking larger amounts/longer than intended; persistent desire/unsuccessful efforts to cut down; great time spent obtaining/using/recovering; craving; failure to fulfill major role obligations; continued use despite social/interpersonal problems; giving up important activities; use in physically hazardous situations; continued use despite physical/psychological problems; tolerance; withdrawal. Severity: mild (2-3), moderate (4-5), severe (6+). Physical dependence diagnosis: withdrawal syndrome upon cessation (opioid withdrawal: rhinorrhea, lacrimation, yawning, piloerection, diarrhea, mydriasis, muscle aches; benzodiazepine withdrawal: anxiety, insomnia, tremor, seizures). Key distinction: patients appropriately using prescribed medications develop dependence but not addiction; addiction involves maladaptive behavioral patterns beyond physiologic adaptation.
